---
pagetitle: "jstats — Troubleshooting"
title-prefix: ""
---

## Troubleshooting

Most problems during setup and early use are one of a handful of things. Find the
symptom that matches yours below and open it for the fix. This page grows over time;
if your problem isn't here, the *Install jstats* page has the install-specific ones
close at hand, and you can always report a new problem by email at
[SurveyCentre@griffith.edu.au](mailto:SurveyCentre@griffith.edu.au) — including the
full error message and your R and jstats versions makes it quicker to sort out.

## Installing jstats

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="API rate limit exceeded"}
GitHub limits how many requests it answers from one internet connection per hour.
A single install is well under the limit, but two situations can trip it: retrying
a failing install many times in quick succession, or several people on the **same
network** (a campus or office connection) all installing within the same hour and
hitting the shared limit together. The fix is to **wait an hour and try again**, and
to avoid rapid repeated retries. This limit goes away entirely once jstats reaches
CRAN, because CRAN installs don't use the GitHub interface at all.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="there is no package called 'remotes'"}
Installing from GitHub needs the **remotes** package, and it isn't installed yet.
Run this first, then retry the install:

```r
install.packages("remotes")
```
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="A build or compilation error"}
This means R tried to build one of jstats's **supporting packages** from source,
which needs build tools you normally don't need for jstats. Work through these in
order — most people never get past the first step.

1. **Answer "no" to the compile prompt.** If R asks whether to *"install from
   sources the package which needs compilation,"* type `n` and press Enter, then
   run the install again. R will fetch a ready-made version of that package
   instead. This resolves the great majority of cases.
2. **Windows — if it still fails:** install the **Rtools** version that matches
   your R (for example **Rtools45** for R 4.5.x) from
   <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/>, run the installer with its
   defaults, then **restart R** (Session menu, Restart R) and try the install
   again.
3. **Mac — if it still fails:** open the **Terminal** app and run
   `xcode-select --install`, follow the prompts, then **restart R** and retry. In
   the rarer case the error specifically mentions `gfortran` or a Fortran
   compiler, the community-maintained **macrtools** package installs the full
   matching toolchain for you — see its instructions at
   <https://mac.thecoatlessprofessor.com/macrtools/>.

Once jstats reaches CRAN, none of this applies — installation becomes a single
ready-made download.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="The install seems half-done or broken"}
The simplest fix is to remove jstats and install it fresh:

```r
remove.packages("jstats")
remotes::install_github("JMA61/jstats", upgrade = "never")
library(jstats)
```
:::

## Starting RStudio and loading jstats

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="RStudio hangs or loops every time it starts"}
This almost always means an **install command was put in your `.Rprofile`**. That
file runs *every time R starts*, so an install line tries to reinstall on every
startup and can loop. Your `.Rprofile` should only ever hold lightweight startup
lines like `library(jstats)` — never an install command. To recover: close RStudio,
open your Project folder in your computer's file manager, open the `.Rprofile` file
in a plain text editor (Notepad on Windows, TextEdit on Mac), delete the install
line so only the `library(jstats)` line remains, save, and reopen RStudio.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="Loading jstats pauses for several seconds on a no-internet connection"}
While jstats is still in development it installs from GitHub, and each time it
loads it briefly checks GitHub for a newer version. The package itself works fully
offline, but on a machine that is **connected to a network yet has no route to the
open internet** -- common on locked-down lab or server installations -- that check
has to time out before loading finishes, so `library(jstats)` can pause for a few
seconds.

To skip the check and load immediately, set this option *before* loading the
package:

```r
options(jstats.check_updates = FALSE)
library(jstats)
```

Putting the `options(...)` line above the `library(jstats)` line in your
`.Rprofile` makes it the default every session. On an ordinary internet
connection you can leave it out -- the check is fast there, and it's how you learn
an update is available.

This is specific to the current GitHub version. Once jstats reaches CRAN,
installing and updating happen the normal CRAN way, with no startup check of this
kind.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="could not find function 'jfreq' (or another jstats function)"}
jstats probably isn't loaded in this session. Run:

```r
library(jstats)
```

If it still isn't found, check spelling and capitalization — R is case-sensitive,
so `jfreq` and `jFreq` are different names.
:::

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true" title="object 'community' not found"}
The shipped datasets become available once the package is loaded. Run
`library(jstats)` first, then try again. (If you're working with your own data
instead, make sure you've loaded it — see
[jstats with your own data](your-data.qmd).)
:::
